coredata.dc:title: Dual-functional CDs@ZIF-8/chitosan luminescent film sensors for simultaneous detection and adsorption of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Miao
Mu, Lan
Cao, Shaomei
Yang, Yuhuan
Feng, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) residues have been noted as an important class of emerging contaminants in the environment, thus fast, straightforward, highly sensitive detection method is becoming an issue that must be addressed. Herein, a novel CDs@ZIF-8 sensing material was fabricated by encapsulating luminescent carbon dots (CDs) into the metal-organic framework (ZIF-8) to achieve highly luminescent porous composites. Furthermore, to avoid the secondary contamination caused by powders, a shapable CDs@ZIF-8/CS film sensor as a portable disposable test strip with dual-function of TC detection and adsorption was designed by crosslinking CDs@ZIF-8 with chitosan (CS). The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8/CS hybrid film exhibited the high sensitivity and selectivity for TC fluorescence detection and rapid decontaminate capability, as well as high optical transmittance and robust mechanical property, which were essential for the practical sensing application. This paper-based dual-functional luminescent sensor exhibits promising practicability for TC detection and contaminant removal in pharmaceutical analysis, food safety and water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZIF-8
Chitosan
Luminescent sensors
Tetracycline antibiotic

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: School of Mechatronic Engineering and Automation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially sponsored by the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality ( 20230742300 and 18595800700 ), Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry, Ministry of Education ( KLRC_ME2103 ), and the project of “joint assignment” in Shanghai University led by Prof. Tongyue Gao from School of Mechatronic Engineering and Automation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image of a single CDs) and (b) XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption (green solid line), PL excitation (red dotted line) and emission spectrum (blue dotted line) of as-obtained CDs. Inset: digital pictures of CDs aqueous solution at visible and UV (365 nm) light. (b) PL spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image and (b) SEM image of ZIF-8; (c) TEM image and (d) SEM image of CDs@ZIF-8; (e) XRD patterns of simulated ZIF-8, the as-synthesized ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8; (f) N2 sorption isotherms of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, respectively.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8.
Typical SEM images of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8/CS weight ratios. (a, b) M-0, (c, d) M-50, (e, f) M-75, and (g, h) M-100.
(a) Optical transmittance and (b) typical stress-strain curves of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8 loadings. Inset: digital picture of the CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-100).
(a) TGA and (b) DSC curves of the CDs@ZIF-8/CS films.

(a) PL emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8 loadings. Inset: photographs of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different shapes under 365 nm UV light. (b) PL emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) with different excited time under 365 nm UV light. Inset: normalized PL intensity with different excited time under 365 nm UV light.

Performances of detection, selectivity and adsorption of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films for TC. (a) Fluorescence quenching of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) in the presence of different concentrations of TC. (b) The selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) to TC and other antibiotics detection. Concentrations of all kinds of antibiotics are used as 60 μM, and the excited wavelength is 380 nm. UV–vis spectra of TC remained in the solution after being adsorbed by (c) CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) and (d) pure CS film (M-0) for different times.
Schematic illustration for the assembly of CDs@ZIF-8/CS dual-function thin-film sensor.

head.ce:title: Dual-functional CDs@ZIF-8/chitosan luminescent film sensors for simultaneous detection and adsorption of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dehong Autonomous Prefecture Institute of Sugar Industry, Yunnan 678400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dehong Autonomous Prefecture Institute of Sugar Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dehong Autonomous Prefecture Institute of Sugar Industry, Yunnan 678400, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) residues have been noted as an important class of emerging contaminants in the environment, thus fast, straightforward, highly sensitive detection method is becoming an issue that must be addressed. Herein, a novel CDs@ZIF-8 sensing material was fabricated by encapsulating luminescent carbon dots (CDs) into the metal-organic framework (ZIF-8) to achieve highly luminescent porous composites. Furthermore, to avoid the secondary contamination caused by powders, a shapable CDs@ZIF-8/CS film sensor as a portable disposable test strip with dual-function of TC detection and adsorption was designed by crosslinking CDs@ZIF-8 with chitosan (CS). The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8/CS hybrid film exhibited the high sensitivity and selectivity for TC fluorescence detection and rapid decontaminate capability, as well as high optical transmittance and robust mechanical property, which were essential for the practical sensing application. This paper-based dual-functional luminescent sensor exhibits promising practicability for TC detection and contaminant removal in pharmaceutical analysis, food safety and water treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical compounds studied in the article
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (PubChem CID: 311)
Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (PubChem CID: 15865313)
2-methylimidazole (PubChem CID: 12749)
Ethylenediamine (PubChem CID: 3301)
Chitosan (PubChem CID: 71853)

Tetracycline (TC) has always been widely used as an effective antibiotic for the therapy of human and animal infections. However, irrational abusing of TC results in excessive levels of residual TC in environmental media such as soil and water (Saadati et al., 2016), and even damages the ecosystem balance which has remarkably facilitated the evolution of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (Wang et al., 2018). Residual TC is considered to be a severe organic pollutant in wastewater and even in foods and drinking water (milk, fish and grains), which may cause the health issues such as hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal disturbance threatening human health (Li, Yang, et al., 2020; Li, Zeng, et al., 2020). Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop effective routes and stratagems to monitor TC residues.

Several analytical methods for the determination of TC have focused on microbiological assay, electrophoresis, gas chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (GC–MS), liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (El Alami El Hassani et al., 2019; Kurittu et al., 2000; Yan et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2015; Zehra et al., 2018). However, most of these methods are time-consuming and rely on complicated instruments and professional trained personnel, which limit their application in routine analysis. Besides, rare attention has been paid on the removal of antibiotic residues in environment. Therefore, the development of multifunctional materials for the rapid detection and removal of TC residues synchronically is of great importance for the treatment of environmental pollution (Ehtesabi et al., 2019; Malik & Iyer, 2017; Tang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2018).

Fluorescence sensors with the advantages of quick sensing, high selectivity and convenient utilization, have proven to be excellent promising candidate in analysis field. To date, TC fluorescence sensors were previously fabricated using carbon dots (CDs) (Ehtesabi et al., 2019), conjugated polyelectrolyte (Malik & Iyer, 2017), molybdenum disulfide nanoplates (Jia et al., 2019) and anthracene-based metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) (Liu et al., 2018). Among these various fluorescence probes, CDs is becoming a prior alternative for TC detection in sensor manufacturing owing to the excellent chemical stability, high biocompatibility, good water solubility as well as the high sensitivity to TC (Miao et al., 2018). More importantly, a variety of functional groups such as epoxy, carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl on the surface of CDs rendered CDs with multifunctionality by interacting with various organic, polymeric and biological species (Ehtesabi et al., 2019; Long et al., 2021; Qin & Yan, 2018). For the simultaneous detection and adsorption of TC residues, the limitations of CDs in water including easy quenching and aggregations by various ions and other chemical substances should be addressed.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are an advanced kind of three-dimensional porous materials with a periodic network (Liu et al., 2022). Due to the adjustable pore size, large specific surface area, high porosity, regular channels and abundant interaction sites, MOFs was designed to be as a potential platform to encapsulate various types of biological molecules and nanoparticles (Fan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Qin & Yan, 2018; Zhao & Li, 2020). At the same time, due to the abundant nano-sized cavities and regular pores, MOFs can be acted as confinement to avoid the aggregation of nanoparticles, in which the nanoparticles were controlled in the pore structure during the synthesis of composite materials. The encapsulation of CDs with high luminescence while substantially maintaining the original frameworks and properties of the MOFs host are an effective approach to fabricate excellent composites which exhibit superior properties to the isolated components (Zhao & Li, 2020). As a subclass of MOFs, zeolite imidazole frameworks (ZIFs) has attracted extensive attentions due to the better chemical stability in aqueous solutions with the environmentally friendly synthesis in room temperature (Ma et al., 2019; Shahrokhian & Ranjbar, 2019). These advantages make it great promising candidate for the detection and adsorption of antibiotics in water (Liu et al., 2019). However, the innate morphologies of MOFs crystals are most commonly found in small-sized powders which hinder their processability and practicability. Additionally, the solution-based testing reagents increase the difficulties in handling during the detection process as well as long-term storage. Therefore, it is an available way to integrate CDs@ZIF-8 powders into a shapeable film sensor with highly-utilizable components to offset the abovementioned defects.

Chitosan (CS), as a polysaccharide biopolymer with active amino and hydroxyl groups, has already been explored in the fabrication of multifunctional composite films with high transparency (Huang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2018). In this work, a robust CDs@ZIF-8/CS films assembled by cross-linking CDs@ZIF-8 with chitosan (CS) polymer is designed using a facile route of one-step thermal evaporation. We hypothesize the possibility of encapsulating CDs into ZIF-8 skeleton by situ-growth process to achieve the synergistic effect. The properties of CDs@ZIF-8 are investigated as luminescent agent and adsorbent for the as-prepared fluorescence sensors. Considering the secondary contamination from powders, we provide the rationality of synthesize CDs@ZIF-8/CS composite film promoted by cross-linking effects between the abundant functional groups of CDs@ZIF-8 and CS, and the effects of CDs@ZIF-8 to CS ratio on structure and properties will be investigated, including transparency, mechanical property and thermal stability, as well as fluorescence, selectivity and adsorption capacity to TC. As far as we know, this is the first time to design a dual-function sensor platform based on CDs@ZIF-8/CS thin-films for simultaneously efficient detection and removal TC in water. Overall, our work provides a valuable reference for fabricating film sensors applied in environmental pollution control and remediation technologies.

All chemicals used were commercially obtained without further purification. Citric acid (99.5%), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 99%), 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, 99%), ethylenediamine (99.5%), chitosan (medium molecular weight, over 95% deacetylated) and acetic acid (99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The luminescent CDs were synthesized using a convenient one-step hydrothermal method as described previously (Zhu et al., 2013). Briefly, citric acid (3.15 g) and ethylenediamine (1 mL) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL). The solution was then transferred into an autoclave (50 mL, Teflon-lined) and heated up to 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the initially colourless solution turned into a dark-brown solution, subsequently, the resultant solution was subjected to dialysis (1000 MWCO) for 12 h to obtain pure CDs. Finally, dry CDs were obtained by lyophilizing the dialyzed solution.

The CDs were introduced into the zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to form CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites through a self-assembly method (Qin et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2016). In a typical experiment, 0.446 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2 mL of the CDs aqueous solution were dissolved in 25 mL of methanol (denoted as solution A), and 0.513 g of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in another 25 mL of methanol (denoted as solution B). Afterwards, solution A was slowly poured into solution B under constantly stirring, and then let stand for 24 h at room temperature to form turbid solution. The products were washed with methanol for three times and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for overnight. As a comparison, ZIF-8 was synthesized without adding of CDs aqueous solution according to the similar procedures.

The assembly of luminescent CDs@ZIF-8/CS films was presented in Scheme 1 . CS was dispersed in a 2% acetic acid solution and stirred for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous CS solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL (Wan et al., 2015). Then, 15 mg of CDs@ZIF-8 was dissolved in 3 mL of the above CS solution under continuously stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, the CDs@ZIF-8/CS suspension was degassed and spread on a flat plate at 50 °C for 3 h to form a transparent film. The CDs@ZIF-8/CS composite films with different weight ratios of CDs@ZIF-8/CS (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 wt%) were peeled from the plate and labeled as M-0, M-25, M-50, M-75, and M-100, respectively. All the composite films were controlled with ~15 μm in thickness and ~45 mm in diameter.

The microstructures were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. The surface morphologies were obtained using a field emission-scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (D/max 2550 V, Rigaku, Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700 spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was collected using the pressed KBr pellet with a scan range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Optical transmittance was measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (2501PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed using a spectrofluorophotometer (FLS-920, Edinburgh, UK). The adsorption-desorption of N2 was investigated at 77 K on a nitrogen adsorption instrument (Autosorb-IQ2, Quantachrome, UK). The samples were degassed at 300 °C for 10 h before measurement. The mechanical properties were recorded at a crosshead speed of 2 mm/min with an Instron 5943 Single Column Tabletop Testing System. The thermal stability was conducted on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, SDT Q600, USA) from 30 to 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a N2 atmosphere.

The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) was cut to a suitable size to accommodate the requirements of the fluorescent cuvette. The sheared M-75 films were then immersed in a series of TC aqueous solutions to detect the fluorescence response using a spectrofluorophotometer (FLS-920, Edinburgh, UK). The emission spectra of M-75 films were recorded from 380 nm to 650 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

For investigating the possible interference, a series of related substances were identified individually, including penicillin (PEN), griseofulvin (GRI), erythromycin (ERY), streptomycin (STR), chloramphenicol (THI) and kanamycin (KANA). The fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) was examined by spectrofluorophotometer (FLS-920, Edinburgh, UK) in the presence of TC and other antibiotics. Concentrations of all kinds of antibiotics are used as 60 μM, and the excited wavelength is 380 nm.

The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) pure CS film (M-0) was immersed into a given initial TC concentrations of 0.1 mM and shaken at room temperature to ensure mixing completely. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2501PC, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the residual TC concentration in the solution at different adsorption time until equilibrium was reached.

From the synthetic illustration of the dual-function thin-film sensors, an effective strategy was designed to embed CDs into 3D porous structure during the in-situ growth of ZIF-8 to achieve highly luminescent CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites (Scheme 1). The encapsulation of CDs within ZIF-8 was conducive to prevent aggregation and draining of CDs, so as to achieve a uniform and well-dispersed CDs-based luminescent film. Significantly, the ZIF-8 hosts with high porosity can adsorb TC to intensify the sensing signal efficiently.

The as-synthesized CDs are predominantly near-spherical nanoparticles with the diameter about 2–3 nm (Fig. 1a), and the HRTEM image of an individual CD (inset in Fig. 1a) further demonstrates that the CDs have high crystallinity with the orderly lattice spacing of approximate 2.1 Å. The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1b) shows a broad (002) peak at around 25° with an interlayer distance of about 0.34 nm, which can be attributed to graphite carbon. From the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1), the broad absorption peaks at 3400–3700 cm−1 and 3200–3600 cm−1 are the stretching vibration peaks of OH and NH, respectively. The 2934 cm−1 band is ascribed to the stretching vibration peak of CH and the 1555 cm−1 band is the bending vibration peak of NH. Besides, the absorption peak at 1444, 1047, and 1655 cm−1 is attributed to CN, CO, and CO, respectively. The results indicate that the surface of CDs obtained by one-step hydrothermal method is rich in carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl and other groups, which is beneficial to the combination with metal ions and also ensures good water solubility.

The optics properties of CDs are illustrated by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 2 ). Two obvious absorption peaks of CDs located at 240 nm and 340 nm are observed, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition (CC) and the n-π* transition (CO) of sp2 C in CDs, respectively (Fig. 2a). The PL spectra show the optimal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths located at 383 nm and 448 nm, respectively. CDs emit bright blue colour under 365 nm UV light corresponding to the emission spectra (inset in Fig. 2a). From the fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2b), when the excitation wavelength is gradually increased from 330 nm to 450 nm, the fluorescence emission peak of CDs is red-shifted from 440 nm to 530 nm. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity reaches a maximum value at 370 nm excitation wavelength, showing obvious excitation-dependent characteristics.

In order to form a novel luminescent nanoplatform, CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were designed by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-8 structure. The formation of CDs@ZIF-8 was considered to the coordination interactions between Zn2+ and the N-containing groups or terminal carboxylic on the CDs surface during ZIF-8 growth. A typical, uniform sodalite zeolite-type structure of ZIF-8 with hexagonal shape is about 200 nm in size (Fig. 3a–d). Interestingly, negligible changes of the morphological evolution from pure ZIF-8 to the CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites are visible, indicating that the introduction of CDs does not affect the pristine morphology of ZIF-8. It is worth noting that the discretely distributed CDs in the outside of CDs@ZIF-8 are not found (Fig. 3c, d). Meanwhile, no significant changes are observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. 3e) after introducing CDs into ZIF-8, further demonstrating the structure stability of ZIF-8. But more than that, the surface area (SBET) of CDs@ZIF-8 is slightly decreased from 1496 m2 g−1 for ZIF-8 to 1390 m2 g−1 (Fig. 3f). Most importantly, the optical properties of the CDs are retained after being encapsulated into ZIF-8 structure (Fig. S2). The emission characteristics of CDs@ZIF-8 aqueous solution are similar to those of CDs, exhibiting bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, which can be clearly discerned by the naked eyes (inset of Fig. S2). The assembly of CDs@ZIF-8 is further confirmed by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a). CDs@ZIF-8 has a clear absorption peak at around 340 nm, corresponding to the absorption peak of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibits an infrared spectrum in agreement with the pure ZIF-8, except the appearance of a characteristic peak at 1655 cm−1, indicating the carboxylic acid functional group on the surface of CDs coordinated with Zn2+ and served as “antenna” for sensitizing the ZIF-8 cations' luminescence (Qin & Yan, 2018). It is noticed that the intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8 characteristic peaks belonging to CDs are weakened or disappeared, which may be due to the fact that CDs are successful embedded in ZIF-8.

The CDs@ZIF-8/CS composite films with layered stacked structure were fabricated via one-step thermal evaporation process. The surface and cross-section SEM images of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8 loadings are shown in Fig. 5 . It can be clearly seen from the surface SEM images that the pure CS film has a flat, smooth and dense surface, while the surface flatness of the composite film slightly decreases after the addition of CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. With the increasing loadings of particulate CDs@ZIF-8 in CS matrix to 75 wt%, the protrusion formed nanoparticles are exposed on film surface. From the cross-sectional SEM images (Figs. 5d, f, h and S3), CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites are uniformly distributed into the layered structure of the CS matrix, which is possibly due to the strong interaction and good compatibility between CS and CDs@ZIF-8. Interestingly, all the CDs@ZIF-8/CS films exhibit excellent transparency in the visible light range, only a slight decrease for the optical transmittance with the increasing additions of CDs@ZIF-8 in comparison with the pristine CS film (M-0) (Fig. 6a), which is probably attributed to the light absorption of CDs@ZIF-8 and light scattering of the relatively rough surface. It is clear that the optical transmittance of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-100) still remained 94% at 550 nm with the maximum addition of CDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles (100 wt%). The background words “Shanghai University” can be clearly identified in the inset of Fig. 6a. Interestingly, it is worth noted that a significant UV shielding existed at 340 nm due to the UV absorption of CDs@ZIF-8. Results indicated that the composite films exhibited very high optical transmittance and excellent UV-blocking properties.

Taking account of the mechanical properties for a film sensor is a vital in practical application, Fig. 6b shows the stress-strain curves of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8 loadings. Compared with CS film (M-0), all the CDs@ZIF-8/CS films display the dramatic enhancement in both tensile strength and elongation at break. The tensile strength of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) can be found to the highest value of 93.18 MPa. The strong dependence of the tensile strength on the loadings of CDs@ZIF-8 is probably related to the strong crosslinking between CDs@ZIF-8 and -OH/-NH2 groups on the surface of CS, in which CDs@ZIF-8 works as a solid heterojunction that can absorb and transfer the fracture energy (Han et al., 2011).

Furthermore, the thermal stability of the CDs@ZIF-8/CS film is another crucial parameter to pave the way for a practical detection application. Fig. 7a shows the TGA curves of the CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with different CDs@ZIF-8 loadings, all the films exhibit similar thermal decomposition behaviors corresponding to the results in DSC curves (Fig. 7b). The first stage ranged from 25 °C to 150 °C is due to the evaporation of water or the remained acetic acid in the films. At the second stage from 150 °C to 230 °C, CS undergoes the break of the molecular chain. The main weight loss from 230 °C to 400 °C is attributed to the depolymerisation and decomposition process of CDs@ZIF-8/CS nanocomposites. As a result, the prominent mechanical properties and thermal stability of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films are beneficial for prolonging the service life of the flexible film sensors.

Derived from fluorescence properties of the illuminant materials of CDs, the CDs@ZIF-8/CS films have the same emission spectra located at 448 nm (Fig. 8a). The variations of fluorescent intensities of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films reveal the strong dependence on the loading of CDs@ZIF-8. It is probably due to the homogeneous distribution of CDs in ZIF-8 cavity that avoids the possible fluorescence quenching caused by agglomeration of CDs. Owing to the outstanding mechanical toughness and flexibility, CDs@ZIF-8/CS film can be easily cut into the complex shapes without crack or fracture, while the bright blue illuminant properties can be clearly maintained (Inset in Fig. 8a). Furthermore, when CDs@ZIF-8/CS film is immersed into water for 30 min, there is no clear deformation can be observed with only a slight decrease about 9% for fluorescent intensity (Fig. S4). This can be strongly proved the interaction between CDs@ZIF-8 and CS as well as the chemical stability of the film in aqueous solutions. The effect of UV radiation time on the fluorescence properties is also investigated (Fig. 8b). The fluorescence intensity declines only 6% after 24 h UV radiation (Inset in Fig. 8b), demonstrating excellent fluorescence stability. Consequently, CDs@ZIF-8/CS film is becoming an ideal candidate for fluorescent tetracycline (TC) detection, because of the excellent mechanical property, thermostability and fluorescence stability.

To assess the potential of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) as a ratiometric sensor, the concentration-dependent photoluminescent properties are investigated (Fig. 9a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film gradually decreased with an increase in TC concentrations. When the concentration of TC is increased to 60 μM, the emission intensity reduces to 9.1%. The corresponding plots of (I0-I)/I0 for the quenching of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film by TC are provided in Fig. S5. The fluorescence quenching is probably deduced as the electron transfer process between CDs@ZIF-8 and TC. Surface functional groups of CDs@ZIF-8, such as carboxylic acid, amino and hydroxyl groups, coordinated with hydroxyl and amide groups of TC to intensify nonradiative recombination of the excitons, leading to the change of the electronic structure of probe and the corresponding fluorescence variations (Long et al., 2021; Qin & Yan, 2018; Tang et al., 2020). Moreover, photoinduced electron transfer may not the only mechanism observed in this system. Fig. S6 reveals UV absorption spectrum of TC and excitation spectrum of CDs have an overlap in the range from 340 nm to 400 nm, leading to a competitive absorption of the source energy between the sensor and the biomarker, which means inner filter effect should also attributed to the quenching performance (Al-Hashimi et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2016).

To evaluate the specificity of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) to TC, a series of assays have been carried out to examine the fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film (M-75) in the presence of TC and other antibiotics, including penicillin (PEN), griseofulvin (GRI), erythromycin (ERY), streptomycin (STR), chloramphenicol (THI) and kanamycin (KANA). As shown in Fig. 9b, the fluorescence of the sensing system shows the tiny changes in all the other antibiotics solution. But when adding 60 μM TC, the fluorescence quenching is dramatically observed. The convinced results demonstrate that the earlier mentioned substances have negligible effect on the fluorescent intensity of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film, indicating that the film sensor has a high selectivity toward TC. The UV–vis absorptions of other analytes are compared with the fluorescent spectrum of CDs, and no overlaps were observed for all of the other antibiotics. This can deduce that IFE is one of quenching mechanism in our sensing system and also explain the high selectivity to TC (Liu et al., 2019).

Significantly, the highlight of CDs@ZIF-8/CS film is proposed to be used as not only detection but also the adsorbing materials for TC. This is the first time for a dual-functional CDs@ZIF-8/CS film to integrate detection and adsorption in one sensing platform. Fig. 9c illustrates the adsorption experiment of CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) to TC (0.1 mM) during different time. A red shift of the absorption spectra of TC can be obviously obtained when adding CDs@ZIF-8/CS films (M-75) to TC solution, while no shift is occurred when adding the pure CS film (M-0) (Fig. 9d). That is attributed to the electrostatic interaction between COO−/OH groups of CDs@ZIF-8 and NH2 groups of TC and consequently the complex is formed. But the binding strength is not strong enough especially in acidic pH environment (Giammarco et al., 2016). Acetic acid was used during the synthesis process so that some of it might be remained in as-prepared CD@ZIF-8/CS films. When CD@ZIF-8/CS film sensor was immersed in TC aqueous solutions, the surface of the film became a weak acidic condition and the electrostatic interactions are suppressed due to association of H+ and COO−, thus some as-formed complexes would be desorbed from the surface of CD@ZIF-8/CS films into TC solution, leading to the red shift of UV–vis absorption. After 180 min, the adsorption equilibrium is reached and the adsorption rate can be calculated as 69%, indicating CDs@ZIF-8/CS films with a good adsorption capability. This dual-functional CDs@ZIF-8/CS films provide a bright prospect for the detection-absorption integrated platform, so as to provide a new strategy for the development of MOF-based sensors.

In summary, a facile in-situ growth of ZIF-8 to encapsulate CDs into the MOF skeleton was successfully developed to yield outstanding blue luminescent CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The resultant CDs@ZIF-8/CS films were achieved by crosslinking CDs@ZIF-8 with CS matrix that displayed distinguished synergetic advantages of high optical transmittance, excellent mechanical robustness and thermal stability. Owing to the remarkable photoluminescence, the CDs@ZIF-8/CS film sensor displayed high sensitivity and selectivity for TC fluorescence detection, exhibiting obviously concentration-dependent quenching mechanism with a detection limit of 0.60 μM. More importantly, the sensing probe with porous structure also showed TC adsorption synchronously to be applied as filter for the rapid decontamination and high removal of TC pollution.

Miao Miao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Lan Mu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shaomei Cao: Investigation. Yuhuan Yang: Investigation. Xin Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially sponsored by the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (20230742300 and 18595800700), Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry, Ministry of Education (KLRC_ME2103), and the project of “joint assignment” in Shanghai University led by Prof. Tongyue Gao from School of Mechatronic Engineering and Automation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.119587.
